key_insight: Ultrafast high-temperature sintering can lock in metastable zirconia phases inside copper while avoiding the grain coarsening and matrix relaxation typical of conventional sintering.
engineering_relevance: This enables stronger, tougher copper-based cermets with refined microstructures, opening pathways for transformation toughening, self-healing, and crack-sensing components.
method: Joule-heated ultrafast sintering to ~900 °C at about 100 °C/s
Very short thermal dwell: 20 s hold
Promotes semi-thermal austenite formation while limiting copper grain growth
evidence_signal: The abstract reports cermets with up to 50 wt% metastable austenite retained at room temperature in a fine-grained, homogeneous microstructure.
watch_item: Phase retention and microstructure control may depend strongly on precise heating rates, dwell time, and sample geometry, which could complicate scale-up.
paper_title: Leveraging rapid sintering to retain metastable zirconia in copper
